document.title: 8. lecture Function analysis II
document.full_text:
Prof. Inese Bula University of Latvia Faculty of Physics, Mathematics and Optometry Lecture Notes «Matemātiskā analīze I» CALCULUS I Doc. Līga Zariņa University of Latvia Faculty of Computing
Dr. Līga Zariņa Researcher University of Latvia Laboratory for Perceptual and Cognitive Systems at the Faculty of Computing Raiņa bulv 19, room 418 E-mail: liga.zarina@lu.lv - please, write to arrange consultations if needed Phone: +371 28622755
LECTURE 8. FUNCTION ANALYSIS II Concavity Local Extrema 8. Homework
Concavity If the tangent line turns steadly in the counterclockwise direction, we say that the graph is concave up; if the tangent turns in the clockwise direction, the graph is concave down. DEFINITION 8.1. Let f be differentiable on an open interval I. We say that f (as well as its graph) is concave up (convex) on I if f’ is increasing on I, and we say that f is concave down on I if f’ is decreasing on I. Concave up – the graph is under the tangent line in the considered interval Concave down – the graph is over the tangent line in the considered interval
Concavity DEFINITION 8.2. Let f be continuous at c. We call (c, f(c)) an inflection point on the graph of f if f is concave up on one side of c and concave down on the other side.
Concavity Let f be differentiable on an open interval I Concave up – for any two points of the interval the graph that connects these two points is over chord that connect these points Concave down – for any two points of the interval the graph that connects these two points is under chord that connect these points
Concavity THEOREM 8.3. (Concavity theorem) Let f be twice differentiable on an open interval I. 1) If f’’(x) < 0 for all x in I, then f is concave up on I. 2) If f’’(x) > 0 for all x in I, then f is concave down on I. Points where f’’(x) = 0 or where f’’(x) does not exist are the candidates for points of inflection. The conclusion is made according the change of signs of the f’’(x).
Concavity Example 1 𝑦 = 3𝑥2 − 𝑥2 𝑥4 − 1 𝑦′′ = 6 𝑥4 Critical points are -1, 0 and 1. Detect the signs of y’’. Function is concave down on (-∞;-1) and (1; +∞) concave up on (-1;0) and (0;1) The points of inflection are (-1, 2) and (1, 2). The point 0 does not belong to Dom(y) and also the change of signd does not indicate 0 as point of inflection.
Concavity Example 𝑦 = (𝑥 + 2)3 𝑥 Please find the points of inflection! 4(𝑥 − 1) 𝑦′′ = 3 9 𝑥5 Critical points are 0 and 1. Detect the signs of y’’. Both critica points (0, 0) and (0, 3) are points of inflection. The point 0 belongs to Dom(y), but at this point does not exist y’’. Function is concave down on (-∞;-1) and (1; +∞) concave up on (0;1)
Concavity Exercises Please find the points of inflection and concavity intervals! 𝑥2 𝑎) 𝑦 = (𝑥 − 1)2 𝑏) 𝑦 = ln(𝑥2 + 2𝑥 − 3)
Local Extrema The maximum and minimum value on a given interval are reffered as global extreme values (global maximum value and global minimum value). DEFINITION 8.6. (Monotonicity theorem) Let S, the domain of f, contain the point c. We say that 1) f(c) is a local maximum value of f if there ia an interval (a, b) containing c such that f(c) is the maximum value of f on (a, b) ∩ S. 2) f(c) is a local minimum value of f if there ia an interval (a, b) containing c such that f(c) is the minimum value of f on (a, b) ∩ S. 3) f(c) is a local extreme value of f if it is either a local minimum or a local maximum value. Any global extreme value is also a local extreme value.
Local Extrema Where do local extreme values occur? The candidates for points where local extreme may occure are critical points: • End points • Stationary points (the derivative = 0) • Singular points (the derivative does not exist) We are not claiming that there must be a local extremum at every critical point. Example 𝑦 = 𝑥 3 , 𝑦 ′ = 3𝑥 2, the only critical point is 0, but here the function does not reach local minimum or local maximum value. However, if the derivative is positive on one side of the critical point and negative on the other (and if the function is continuous), then we have alocal extremum.
Local Extrema THEOREM 8.7. (First derivative test) Let f be continuous on an open interval (a, b) that contains a critical point c. 1) If f’(x) > 0 for all x in (a, c) and f’(x) < 0 for all x in (c, b), then f(c) is a local maximum value of f. 2) If f’(x) < 0 for all x in (a, c) and f’(x) > 0 for all x in (c, b), then f(c) is a local minimum value of f. 3) If f’(x) has the same sign on both sides of c, then f(c) is not a local extreme value of f.
Local Extrema Proof of 1). □ Since f’(x) > 0 for all x in (a, c), f is increasing on (a, c] by the Monotonicity theorem. And again, since f’(x) < 0 for all x in (a, c), f is decreasing on [c, b) by the Monotonicity theorem. Thus, f(x) < f(c) for all x in (a, b), except of course at x = c. We conclude that f(c) is a local maximum. ■
Local Extrema Exercise 4𝑥 + 5 𝑓 𝑥 = 1 − 𝑥2 Critical points are -2, -1/2, -1 and 1. At -1 and 1 there is no extrema values, because the derivative values do not change signs. Also these values do not belong to Dom(f(x)). According the change of signs at the point -2 the function has local maximum value 1 and at the -1/2 the function has local minimum value 4. We see that these are local extrema values, because the maximum value is smaller then minimum value.
Local Extrema Exercise 3 𝑥 + 4 𝑓 𝑥 = 𝑥2 Please find the extrema values! 3 𝑥 − 8 ′ 𝑦 = 𝑥3 Critical points are 0 and 2. Hav to detect signd for first derivative. At 0 the function is not defined, thus it cannot have extrema value there, even if the derivative changes the sign. The point (2, 3) in accordance with the change of signs is a local minimum point. It is local, because, for example) f(-2) = -1 < 3.t
Concavity Exercises Please find the extreme values! Please detect if any of them is the global extreme value! 𝑙𝑛𝑥 𝑎) 𝑦 = 𝑥 𝑥𝑛 𝑏) 𝑦 = , 𝑛 = 1, 2, … , 1 + 𝑥2
HOMEWORK 8 𝑥 15. Where is 𝑦 = concave up and where it is concave down? Please, (𝑥−2)2 detect th points of inflection. Answer: point of inflection (-4; -1/9) oncave down on (-4;2) and (2; +∞) concave up on (- ∞;-4) 16. The the number a in the sum with the square of the number b gives 48. Please, find such a and b, so that their multiplication is greatest. Answer: a=32, b=4 Reading: D. Varberg, E. J. Purcell, S. E. Rigdon Calculus. 9th Edition, Prentice-Hall, Inc.m, 2014, p. 155– 166. Chapter 3. Sections 3.2., 3.3.
